questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: adjust
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-able' to 'adjust' forms the adjective 'adjustable', meaning 'capable of being adjusted'. The suffix '-able' is used because it means 'capable of' or 'can be done', which fits here — something adjustable can be adjusted. Other suffixes would not work: 'adjustful' is not a word, 'adjustive' is not standard, and 'adjustous' does not exist. The suffix '-able' specifically attaches to verbs to mean 'can be [verb]ed' — washable, breakable, readable.

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: attract
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-ive' to 'attract' forms the adjective 'attractive', meaning 'pleasing or appealing in appearance'. The suffix '-ive' is used because it means 'having the quality of' or 'tending to' — something attractive has the quality of attracting people. Other suffixes would not work here: 'attractable' would mean 'capable of being attracted' (different meaning), 'attractful' is not a word, and 'attractous' does not exist. The suffix '-ive' specifically conveys the quality of doing the action — creative (creates), expensive (costs a lot).

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: power
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-ful' to 'power' forms the adjective 'powerful', meaning 'having great strength or influence'. The suffix '-ful' means 'full of' — something powerful is full of power. Other suffixes would give different or incorrect meanings: 'powery' is not a standard word, 'powerous' does not exist, and 'powerless' means the opposite ('without power'). The suffix '-ful' is commonly used with nouns to create adjectives meaning 'full of that quality' — hopeful (full of hope), careful (full of care), beautiful (full of beauty).

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: health
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-y' to 'health' forms the adjective 'healthy', meaning 'in good physical condition'. The suffix '-y' means 'having the quality of' or 'characterized by' — someone healthy has health. Other suffixes would not work as naturally: 'healthful' exists but means 'good for your health' (slightly different meaning — healthful food vs a healthy person), 'healthsome' is archaic, and 'healthive' does not exist. The suffix '-y' is common with nouns to mean 'having that quality' — wealthy, dirty, rainy, sunny.

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: nation
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-al' to 'nation' forms the adjective 'national', meaning 'relating to or belonging to a nation'. The suffix '-al' means 'relating to' or 'connected with' — something national relates to a nation. Other suffixes would not work: 'nationful' is not a word, 'nationy' does not exist, and 'nationous' is not standard. The suffix '-al' commonly attaches to nouns to create adjectives of relation — musical (relating to music), personal (relating to a person), emotional (relating to emotion).

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: hope
questions.explanation: Two valid adjectives can be formed from 'hope'. Adding '-ful' gives 'hopeful' (full of hope, feeling optimistic), and adding '-less' gives 'hopeless' (without hope, feeling no optimism). These two suffixes create opposite meanings: '-ful' means 'full of' while '-less' means 'without'. Other suffixes would not work: 'hopey' is not a word, 'hopic' does not exist, and 'hopive' is not standard. Many nouns can take both '-ful' and '-less' to create adjective pairs with opposite meanings — careful/careless, thankful/thankless, peaceful/peaceless.

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: adventure
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-ous' to 'adventure' (dropping the final 'e') forms the adjective 'adventurous', meaning 'willing to take risks or try new things'. The suffix '-ous' means 'full of' or 'having the quality of' — someone adventurous is full of the spirit of adventure. Note the spelling change: the 'e' at the end of 'adventure' is dropped before adding '-ous'. Other suffixes would not work: 'adventureful' is not standard, 'adventurey' does not exist, and 'adventurable' would mean 'capable of being adventured' which makes no sense. The suffix '-ous' is common — famous, dangerous, courageous.

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: hunger
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-y' to 'hunger' (with a spelling change — dropping the 'er' ending) forms the adjective 'hungry', meaning 'feeling a need for food'. The suffix '-y' means 'having the quality of' — someone hungry has hunger. Note the spelling change: 'hunger' does not simply become 'hungery'; instead, the 'er' is replaced with '-y' to form 'hungry'. Other suffixes would not work: 'hungerful' is not a word, 'hungerous' does not exist, and 'hungerial' is not standard. Similar pattern: anger → angry, thirst → thirsty.

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: hero
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-ic' to 'hero' forms the adjective 'heroic', meaning 'showing great bravery or courage'. The suffix '-ic' means 'relating to' or 'having the characteristics of' — something heroic has the characteristics of a hero. Other suffixes would not work: 'heroful' is not a word, 'heroish' is not standard, and 'heroable' does not exist. The suffix '-ic' commonly attaches to nouns to form adjectives — historic (from history), artistic (from artist), poetic (from poet), energetic (from energy).

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: terror
questions.explanation: Two valid adjectives can be formed from 'terror'. Adding '-ible' (with spelling change) gives 'terrible' (extremely bad or frightening), and adding '-ifying' gives 'terrifying' (causing extreme fear). These have slightly different uses: 'terrible' is a general adjective meaning very bad ('terrible weather'), while 'terrifying' specifically means causing terror ('a terrifying experience'). Other forms like 'terrorful' or 'terrorish' do not exist. Note: 'terrific' also comes from 'terror' originally, but today it means 'excellent' — a completely different meaning — so it would be misleading as an answer.

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: quarrel
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-some' to 'quarrel' forms the adjective 'quarrelsome', meaning 'inclined to argue or fight'. The suffix '-some' means 'tending to' or 'inclined to' — someone quarrelsome tends to quarrel. Other suffixes would not work: 'quarrelful' is not standard, 'quarrely' does not exist, and 'quarreling' would be a participle, not a standard adjective. The suffix '-some' is relatively rare but creates useful adjectives — troublesome (causing trouble), handsome, tiresome (causing tiredness), awesome (inspiring awe).

questions.question: Make an adjective from the word: educate
questions.explanation: Two valid adjectives can be formed. Adding '-ional' (via the noun 'education') gives 'educational', meaning 'relating to education'. Adding '-ive' gives 'educative', meaning 'providing education or having an educating effect'. The difference: 'educational' relates to education in general ('educational institution', 'educational policy'), while 'educative' emphasizes the act of teaching ('educative experience'). Other suffixes would not work: 'educateful' is not a word, 'educaty' does not exist. Note the formation path: educate → education → educational (the suffix '-ional' attaches to the noun form, not directly to the verb).

questions.question:
[Participles as Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct -ing, -ed, or -en form:
The ______ (surprise) thing about the weather was the sudden drop in temperature.
questions.explanation: We use the present participle 'surprising' (-ing form) because the adjective describes the thing that CAUSES the feeling. The weather event is the cause — it surprises people. The rule is: use '-ing' when the noun causes the emotion, use '-ed' when the noun experiences the emotion. We cannot use 'surprised' here because 'surprised' describes the person who FEELS surprise, not the thing that causes it. A 'surprised thing' would mean the thing itself felt surprised, which makes no sense — things do not feel emotions. Compare: 'The surprising news' (the news causes surprise) vs. 'The surprised audience' (the audience feels surprise).

questions.question:
[Participles as Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct -ing, -ed, or -en form:
The audience was ______ (please) with my performance.
questions.explanation: We use the past participle 'pleased' (-ed form) because the adjective describes how the audience FELT — they experienced the emotion of pleasure. The rule is: use '-ed' when describing the person or thing that experiences the emotion. We cannot use 'pleasing' here because 'pleasing' would describe something that CAUSES pleasure, not something that FEELS pleasure. 'The audience was pleasing' would mean the audience was causing pleasure to someone else, which is not the intended meaning. The performance was pleasing (it caused pleasure); the audience was pleased (they felt pleasure).

questions.question:
[Participles as Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct -ing, -ed, or -en form:
All ______ (interest) students are requested to contact the office.
questions.explanation: We use the past participle 'interested' (-ed form) because the adjective describes how the students FEEL — they have developed an interest and they experience the feeling of being interested. We cannot use 'interesting' here because 'interesting students' would mean students who CAUSE interest in others (students who are fascinating to look at or talk to). That is a completely different meaning from the intended one. The sentence means students who feel interested in something should contact the office. Compare: 'interested students' (students who feel interest) vs. 'interesting students' (students who fascinate others).

questions.question:
[Participles as Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct -ing, -ed, or -en form:
We enjoyed the ______ (refresh) breeze.
questions.explanation: We use the present participle 'refreshing' (-ing form) because the adjective describes the EFFECT the breeze has on people — the breeze causes a feeling of freshness. The breeze is the cause of the refreshment. We cannot use 'refreshed' here because 'refreshed' describes the person who FEELS refreshed, not the thing that causes the feeling. 'A refreshed breeze' would mean the breeze itself felt refreshed, which makes no sense — a breeze cannot feel emotions. The breeze is refreshing (it causes freshness); the people are refreshed (they feel freshness).

questions.question:
[Participles as Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct -ing, -ed, or -en form:
The father was upset by his child's ______ (annoy) behaviour.
questions.explanation: We use the present participle 'annoying' (-ing form) because the adjective describes the behaviour that CAUSES annoyance. The behaviour is the source of irritation — it annoys the father. We cannot use 'annoyed' here because 'annoyed behaviour' would mean the behaviour itself felt annoyed, which does not make sense. Behaviour cannot feel emotions — it can only cause them. The father is annoyed (he feels the irritation); the behaviour is annoying (it causes the irritation). Remember: the cause takes '-ing', the feeler takes '-ed'.

questions.question:
[Participles as Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct -ing, -ed, or -en form:
The grapes were very ______ (tempt) for the fox.
questions.explanation: We use the present participle 'tempting' (-ing form) because the adjective describes the grapes that CAUSE temptation. The grapes are the source — they create a feeling of desire in the fox. We cannot use 'tempted' here because 'tempted' describes the one who FEELS temptation, not the thing that causes it. 'Tempted grapes' would mean the grapes felt tempted, which is absurd — grapes cannot feel desire. The fox might feel tempted (experiencing the emotion), but the grapes are tempting (causing the emotion).

questions.question:
[Participles as Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct -ing, -ed, or -en form:
Raghav was ______ (disappoint) at being caught.
questions.explanation: We use the past participle 'disappointed' (-ed form) because the adjective describes how Raghav FELT — he experienced the emotion of disappointment. Raghav is a person who feels the emotion. We cannot use 'disappointing' here because 'disappointing' describes something that CAUSES disappointment in others. 'Raghav was disappointing' would mean Raghav was causing disappointment to someone else (like his parents), which is a completely different meaning. The sentence is about Raghav's own feelings about being caught, so '-ed' is correct.

questions.question:
[Participles as Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct -ing, -ed, or -en form:
We spotted a ______ (mystify) object.
questions.explanation: We use the present participle 'mystifying' (-ing form) because the adjective describes the object that CAUSES mystery or confusion. The object is the source — it puzzles the observers. We cannot use 'mystified' here because 'mystified' describes someone who FEELS confused or puzzled. 'A mystified object' would mean the object itself felt confused, which is impossible — objects cannot feel emotions. The observers are mystified (they feel puzzled); the object is mystifying (it causes the puzzlement).

questions.question:
[Attributive vs Predicative Adjectives] Rearrange the words to make a meaningful sentence and state whether the adjective is attributive or predicative:
tastes medicine horrible the
questions.explanation: The adjective 'horrible' is predicative because it comes AFTER the linking verb 'tastes' and describes the subject 'medicine'. Predicative adjectives appear after linking verbs (is, was, seems, tastes, sounds, smells, feels, looks, appears, becomes) and describe the subject through the verb. It is NOT attributive because attributive adjectives come DIRECTLY BEFORE the noun they describe (e.g., 'the horrible medicine'). Here, 'horrible' does not sit before any noun — it sits after the linking verb 'tastes'. If we rewrote this as 'The horrible medicine was rejected', then 'horrible' would be attributive.

questions.question:
[Attributive vs Predicative Adjectives] Rearrange the words to make a meaningful sentence and state whether the adjective is attributive or predicative:
a theorem it was difficult
questions.explanation: The adjective 'difficult' is attributive because it comes DIRECTLY BEFORE the noun 'theorem' and modifies it. Attributive adjectives are placed immediately before the noun they describe. It is NOT predicative because predicative adjectives come after linking verbs and describe the subject. Here, 'difficult' sits before the noun 'theorem', not after a linking verb. If we rewrote this as 'The theorem was difficult', then 'difficult' would be predicative because it would come after the linking verb 'was'.

questions.question:
[Attributive vs Predicative Adjectives] Rearrange the words to make a meaningful sentence and state whether the adjective is attributive or predicative:
cheat honest a nobody would friend
questions.answer: Nobody would cheat an honest friend. (attributive)
questions.explanation: The adjective 'honest' is attributive because it comes DIRECTLY BEFORE the noun 'friend' and modifies it — it tells us what kind of friend. It is NOT predicative because it does not appear after a linking verb. There is no linking verb in this sentence — 'would cheat' is an action verb, not a linking verb. If we rewrote this as 'The friend is honest', then 'honest' would be predicative because it would come after the linking verb 'is'.

questions.question:
[Attributive vs Predicative Adjectives] Rearrange the words to make a meaningful sentence and state whether the adjective is attributive or predicative:
familiar the sounds my ears music to
questions.answer: The music sounds familiar to my ears. (predicative)
questions.explanation: The adjective 'familiar' is predicative because it comes AFTER the linking verb 'sounds' and describes the subject 'music'. The verb 'sounds' here is a linking verb (it connects the subject to a description), not an action verb. It is NOT attributive because 'familiar' does not appear directly before a noun — it appears after the linking verb. If we rewrote this as 'The familiar music played softly', then 'familiar' would be attributive because it would sit directly before the noun 'music'.

questions.question:
[Attributive vs Predicative Adjectives] Rearrange the words to make a meaningful sentence and state whether the adjective is attributive or predicative:
cake the in smells the delicious baking dish
questions.answer: The cake baking in the dish smells delicious. (predicative)
questions.explanation: The adjective 'delicious' is predicative because it comes AFTER the linking verb 'smells' and describes the subject 'cake'. The verb 'smells' here is a linking verb — it connects the subject (cake) to the describing word (delicious). It is NOT attributive because 'delicious' does not sit directly before a noun — it sits after the linking verb. If we rewrote this as 'The delicious cake was baking in the dish', then 'delicious' would be attributive because it would come directly before the noun 'cake'. Note: 'baking' here is a participle describing the cake, not the main adjective.

questions.question:
[Attributive vs Predicative Adjectives] Rearrange the words to make a meaningful sentence and state whether the adjective is attributive or predicative:
incomplete submit not do work
questions.explanation: The adjective 'incomplete' is attributive because it comes DIRECTLY BEFORE the noun 'work' and modifies it — it tells us what kind of work should not be submitted. It is NOT predicative because it does not appear after a linking verb. There is no linking verb in this sentence — 'submit' is an action verb. If we rewrote this as 'The work is incomplete', then 'incomplete' would be predicative because it would come after the linking verb 'is'.

questions.question:
[Attributive vs Predicative Adjectives] Rearrange the words to make a meaningful sentence and state whether the adjective is attributive or predicative:
in early hostel active students a get
questions.answer: Active students in a hostel get up early. (attributive)
questions.explanation: The adjective 'active' is attributive because it comes DIRECTLY BEFORE the noun 'students' and modifies it — it tells us what kind of students. It is NOT predicative because it does not appear after a linking verb — 'get up' is an action verb (phrasal verb), not a linking verb. Important note: 'early' in this sentence functions as an adverb modifying the verb 'get up' (it tells us WHEN they get up), NOT as an adjective. If we rewrote this as 'The students are active', then 'active' would be predicative because it would come after the linking verb 'are'.

questions.question:
[Attributive vs Predicative Adjectives] Rearrange the words to make a meaningful sentence and state whether the adjective is attributive or predicative:
gifted camera a digital aunt my me
questions.answer: My aunt gifted me a digital camera. (attributive)
questions.explanation: The adjective 'digital' is attributive because it comes DIRECTLY BEFORE the noun 'camera' and modifies it — it tells us what kind of camera. It is NOT predicative because it does not appear after a linking verb — 'gifted' here is an action verb meaning 'gave as a gift', not a linking verb. If we rewrote this as 'The camera is digital', then 'digital' would be predicative because it would come after the linking verb 'is'.

questions.question:
[Kinds of Adjectives] Underline the adjectives and identify their kind:
Our India has a rich historical past.
questions.answer: rich (adjective of quality), historical (adjective of quality)
questions.explanation: 'Rich' is an adjective of quality because it describes a characteristic or nature of the past — it tells us WHAT KIND of past India has. It is not an adjective of number (it does not tell us how many), not a demonstrative adjective (it does not point out which one), and not a possessive adjective (it does not show ownership). 'Historical' is also an adjective of quality because it describes the TYPE of past — it tells us what kind of past. It is not a numeral adjective or a demonstrative. Note: 'Our' is a possessive adjective (it shows ownership — the India belonging to us), but the question focuses on the quality adjectives.

questions.question:
[Kinds of Adjectives] Underline the adjectives and identify their kind:
India derives its name from River Indus.
questions.explanation: 'Its' is a possessive adjective because it shows ownership or belonging — it tells us WHOSE name is being discussed (India's name). Possessive adjectives (my, your, his, her, its, our, their) always come before a noun and show possession. 'Its' is NOT an adjective of quality (it does not describe a characteristic), NOT an adjective of number (it does not tell how many), and NOT a demonstrative adjective (it does not point out which one — that would be 'this' or 'that'). There are no other adjectives in this sentence — 'River' and 'Indus' are proper nouns.

questions.question:
[Kinds of Adjectives] Underline the adjectives and identify their kind:
The fertile plains of the Indus were a prosperous region.
questions.answer: fertile (adjective of quality), prosperous (adjective of quality)
questions.explanation: 'Fertile' is an adjective of quality because it describes a characteristic of the plains — it tells us WHAT KIND of plains they were (rich in nutrients, good for farming). It is not a numeral, demonstrative, or possessive adjective. 'Prosperous' is also an adjective of quality because it describes the nature of the region — it tells us what kind of region (wealthy, thriving). It is not a numeral adjective (it does not tell how many), not a demonstrative (it does not point out which one), and not a possessive (it does not show ownership). Both words answer the question 'What kind of?' which is the hallmark of quality adjectives.

questions.question:
[Kinds of Adjectives] Underline the adjectives and identify their kind:
Many ancient civilisations came up on the banks of this river.
questions.answer: Many (adjective of number), ancient (adjective of quality), this (demonstrative adjective)
questions.explanation: 'Many' is an adjective of number (indefinite numeral) because it tells us an approximate QUANTITY of civilisations — how many civilisations. It is an indefinite numeral because it does not give an exact number. It is not a quality adjective because it does not describe what kind of civilisations they were. 'Ancient' is an adjective of quality because it describes the NATURE or characteristic of the civilisations — it tells us what kind they were (old, from long ago). It does not tell us how many or which one. 'This' is a demonstrative adjective because it POINTS OUT which specific river is being referred to. Demonstrative adjectives (this, that, these, those) always point to a specific noun. 'This' is not a quality adjective (it does not describe a characteristic) and not a numeral (it does not tell how many).

questions.question:
[Kinds of Adjectives] Underline the adjectives and identify their kind:
The early settlers lived a nomadic life, hunting and gathering food.
questions.answer: early (adjective of quality), nomadic (adjective of quality)
questions.explanation: 'Early' is an adjective of quality because it describes a characteristic of the settlers — it tells us WHAT KIND of settlers (those who came first, from the initial period). It is not an adjective of number (it does not tell how many settlers) and not a demonstrative (it does not point out which ones specifically). 'Nomadic' is an adjective of quality because it describes the TYPE of life — it tells us what kind of life they lived (moving from place to place without a fixed home). It does not tell us how many lives or which life. Both adjectives answer the question 'What kind of?' Note: 'hunting' and 'gathering' are participles acting as verbs here, not as adjectives.

questions.question:
[Kinds of Adjectives] Underline the adjectives and identify their kind:
Over a period of time, many people began to settle in permanent places in villages.
questions.answer: many (adjective of number), permanent (adjective of quality)
questions.explanation: 'Many' is an adjective of number (indefinite numeral) because it tells us an approximate QUANTITY of people — how many people began to settle. It does not give an exact number, so it is an indefinite numeral adjective. It is not a quality adjective because it does not describe what kind of people they were. 'Permanent' is an adjective of quality because it describes the NATURE of the places — it tells us what kind of places they were (lasting, not temporary). It is not a numeral adjective (it does not tell how many places) and not a demonstrative (it does not point out which specific places). Other indefinite numeral adjectives include: few, some, several, all, no.

questions.question:
[Kinds of Adjectives] Underline the adjectives and identify their kind:
These villages grew into big cities, creating one of the world's earliest civilisations.
questions.answer: These (demonstrative adjective), big (adjective of quality), earliest (adjective of quality, superlative degree)
questions.explanation: 'These' is a demonstrative adjective because it POINTS OUT which specific villages are being discussed — the ones mentioned in the previous sentences. Demonstrative adjectives (this, that, these, those) always identify a specific noun. It is not a quality adjective (it does not describe a characteristic of the villages) and not a numeral (it does not tell how many). 'Big' is an adjective of quality because it describes the SIZE or characteristic of the cities — it tells us what kind of cities. It is not a numeral or demonstrative. 'Earliest' is an adjective of quality in the superlative degree because it describes a characteristic — how old the civilisations were compared to all others. The superlative '-est' form shows it is the highest degree of the quality 'early'. It is not a numeral adjective even though it might seem related to time.

questions.question:
[Kinds of Adjectives] Underline the adjectives and identify their kind:
Today, India is the seventh largest country in the world by land area with the largest population in the world.
questions.answer: seventh (adjective of number), largest (adjective of quality, superlative degree), largest (adjective of quality, superlative degree)
questions.explanation: 'Seventh' is an adjective of number (definite numeral, ordinal) because it tells us India's exact RANK or position — it gives a specific number in a sequence. Ordinal numerals (first, second, third... seventh) tell us the position of something. It is not a quality adjective because it does not describe a characteristic — it tells us a ranking. The two instances of 'largest' are adjectives of quality in the superlative degree because they describe characteristics — the size of the country and the size of the population. They use the superlative form (-est) to show the highest degree compared to all others. 'Largest' is not a numeral adjective because it describes a quality (size), even though it involves comparison.

questions.question:
[Kinds of Adjectives] Underline the adjectives and identify their kind:
It is a democratic country.
questions.explanation: 'Democratic' is an adjective of quality because it describes the NATURE or type of the country — it tells us what kind of country India is (one that is governed by the people through elections). It answers the question 'What kind of country?' It is NOT an adjective of number (it does not tell how many countries), NOT a demonstrative adjective (it does not point out which country — that would be 'this' or 'that'), and NOT a possessive adjective (it does not show ownership). Quality adjectives describe the characteristics, nature, or qualities of a noun.

questions.question:
[Kinds of Adjectives] Underline the adjectives and identify their kind:
India is the only country in the world where both the tiger and the lion are found.
questions.explanation: 'Only' is an adjective of quality because it describes a unique CHARACTERISTIC of the country — it tells us something special about India (that it is the sole country with this feature). It answers the question 'What kind of country?' It is NOT an adjective of number, even though 'only' might seem to suggest 'one' — here it describes a quality of uniqueness rather than counting. It is NOT a demonstrative adjective (it does not point out which country) and NOT a possessive adjective (it does not show ownership). Note: 'both' in this sentence acts as a predeterminer for 'the tiger and the lion', not as the main adjective being identified.

questions.question:
[Order of Adjectives] Write two more adjectives for the category: Opinion/Quality
Given examples: honest, rare
questions.explanation: Opinion/Quality adjectives express a personal judgement or evaluation about a noun — they tell us what we THINK about something. 'Beautiful' and 'wonderful' both express opinions. In the order of adjectives, opinion adjectives come FIRST, before all other types. They cannot be placed in other categories: they are not size words (like 'big'), not shape words (like 'round'), not colour words (like 'red'), and not material words (like 'wooden'). The key test: if the adjective is subjective (different people might disagree), it is an opinion adjective. Other examples: lovely, excellent, terrible, brilliant, ugly.

questions.question:
[Order of Adjectives] Write two more adjectives for the category: Size
Given examples: tiny, large
questions.explanation: Size adjectives describe HOW BIG or HOW SMALL something is — they are objective and measurable. 'Enormous' (very big) and 'miniature' (very small) both describe size. In the order of adjectives, size comes SECOND, after opinion but before physical quality, shape, age, colour, origin, material, and purpose. They cannot be placed in other categories: they are not opinions (size can be measured), not shapes (like 'round'), not colours, and not materials. The key test: if the adjective answers 'How big?' or 'How small?', it is a size adjective. Other examples: huge, small, tall, short, massive, gigantic.

questions.question:
[Order of Adjectives] Write two more adjectives for the category: Physical Quality
Given examples: untidy, smooth
questions.explanation: Physical quality adjectives describe the TEXTURE, CONDITION, or PHYSICAL CHARACTERISTICS of a noun — how something feels or its physical state. 'Rough' (uneven texture) and 'clean' (free from dirt) both describe physical qualities. In the order of adjectives, physical quality comes THIRD, after opinion and size. They cannot be placed in other categories: 'rough' is not a size (it describes texture), 'clean' is not a colour or shape. The key test: if the adjective describes how something physically feels or its condition, it is a physical quality adjective. Other examples: soft, hard, wet, dry, thick, thin, warm, cold.

questions.question:
[Order of Adjectives] Write two more adjectives for the category: Shape
Given examples: spherical, square
questions.explanation: Shape adjectives describe the FORM, OUTLINE, or GEOMETRIC SHAPE of a noun. 'Circular' (like a circle) and 'rectangular' (like a rectangle) both describe shapes. In the order of adjectives, shape comes FOURTH, after opinion, size, and physical quality. They cannot be placed in other categories: 'circular' is not a size (a circle can be any size), not a physical quality (it describes form, not texture), and not a colour. The key test: if the adjective describes what geometric form something has, it is a shape adjective. Other examples: triangular, oval, flat, round, hexagonal, curved.

questions.question:
[Order of Adjectives] Write two more adjectives for the category: Age
Given examples: ancient, new
questions.explanation: Age adjectives describe HOW OLD or HOW NEW something is. 'Modern' (recent, current era) and 'old' (having existed for a long time) both describe age. In the order of adjectives, age comes FIFTH, after opinion, size, physical quality, and shape. They cannot be placed in other categories: 'old' is not an opinion (age can be measured), not a size, not a shape, and not a colour. The key test: if the adjective answers 'How old?' or 'How new?', it is an age adjective. Other examples: young, elderly, brand-new, antique, recent, vintage.

questions.question:
[Order of Adjectives] Write two more adjectives for the category: Colour
Given examples: brown, tan
questions.explanation: Colour adjectives describe the COLOUR or HUE of a noun. 'Crimson' (deep red) and 'silver' (metallic grey) both describe colours. In the order of adjectives, colour comes SIXTH, after opinion, size, physical quality, shape, and age. They cannot be placed in other categories: 'crimson' is not an opinion (it is an objective colour), not a size, not a shape, and not a material (it describes the colour, not what something is made of). The key test: if the adjective names a colour or shade, it is a colour adjective. Other examples: red, blue, golden, emerald, scarlet, azure, maroon.

questions.question:
[Order of Adjectives] Write two more adjectives for the category: Source/Origin
Given examples: Chinese, Arabic
questions.explanation: Source/Origin adjectives describe WHERE something comes from — its geographical or cultural origin. 'Indian' (from India) and 'French' (from France) both describe origin. In the order of adjectives, origin comes SEVENTH, after opinion, size, physical quality, shape, age, and colour. They are usually proper adjectives (capitalized) derived from proper nouns (country/place names). They cannot be placed in other categories: 'Indian' is not a quality opinion, not a size, not a colour, and not a material. The key test: if the adjective tells where something is from, it is an origin adjective. Other examples: Japanese, Italian, British, American, Tamil.

questions.question:
[Order of Adjectives] Write two more adjectives for the category: Material
Given examples: metal, synthetic
questions.explanation: Material adjectives describe WHAT SOMETHING IS MADE OF. 'Wooden' (made of wood) and 'cotton' (made of cotton) both describe the material. In the order of adjectives, material comes EIGHTH, after opinion, size, physical quality, shape, age, colour, and origin, but before purpose. They cannot be placed in other categories: 'wooden' is not a physical quality (it tells what something is made of, not how it feels), not an origin (it does not tell where something is from), and not a colour. The key test: if the adjective tells what something is made from, it is a material adjective. Other examples: leather, silk, plastic, glass, rubber, stone, woollen.

questions.question:
[Order of Adjectives] Write two more adjectives for the category: Purpose
Given examples: wiping, sewing
questions.explanation: Purpose adjectives describe WHAT SOMETHING IS USED FOR. 'Running' (as in running shoes — shoes used for running) and 'cooking' (as in cooking oil — oil used for cooking) both describe the purpose or function. In the order of adjectives, purpose comes LAST (ninth), just before the noun, after all other adjective types. They are often formed from verbs in the '-ing' form. They cannot be placed in other categories: 'running' here is not an opinion, size, or material — it describes what the thing is used for. The key test: if the adjective tells what something is used for or designed to do, it is a purpose adjective. Other examples: sleeping (bag), washing (machine), writing (desk), swimming (pool).

questions.question:
[Order of Adjectives Game] Arrange the adjectives in the correct order before the noun 'urn':
beautiful, big, cylindrical, old, beige, Egyptian, earthen
questions.answer: the beautiful big cylindrical old beige Egyptian earthen urn
questions.explanation: The correct order follows the standard rule: Opinion → Size → Shape → Age → Colour → Origin → Material → Noun. Here: 'beautiful' is Opinion (a personal judgement — it cannot go in size, shape, or any other slot because it is subjective). 'Big' is Size (it tells how large — it cannot be an opinion because size is measurable). 'Cylindrical' is Shape (it describes the form — it cannot be a size or age word). 'Old' is Age (it tells how old — it cannot be a shape or colour). 'Beige' is Colour (it names a shade — it cannot be age or material). 'Egyptian' is Origin (it tells where it is from — it cannot be a colour or material). 'Earthen' is Material (it tells what the urn is made of — it must come last, just before the noun). If any adjective were moved out of this order, it would sound unnatural in English. Remember the sequence: Opinion, Size, Physical Quality, Shape, Age, Colour, Origin, Material, Purpose, Noun.

questions.question:
[Degrees of Comparison - Temperature Chart] Temperature chart: Sun=38°C, Mon=33°C, Tue=30°C, Wed=29°C, Thu=32°C, Fri=35°C, Sat=38°C

Which day was as hot as Sunday?
questions.answer: Saturday was as hot as Sunday. Both had 38°C. (positive degree)
questions.explanation: This uses the positive degree of comparison with the structure 'as ... as'. The positive degree is used to show that two things are EQUAL — they are the same. Both Sunday and Saturday recorded 38°C, so they are equally hot. We cannot use the comparative degree ('hotter than') here because the question asks which day was AS hot, not which was hotter — the two days are equal, not different. We cannot use the superlative degree ('hottest') here because the question is about equality between two days, not about finding the highest among all. The positive degree pattern is: X is as [adjective] as Y.

questions.question:
[Degrees of Comparison - Temperature Chart] Temperature chart: Sun=38°C, Mon=33°C, Tue=30°C, Wed=29°C, Thu=32°C, Fri=35°C, Sat=38°C

Which day was the coolest?
questions.answer: Wednesday was the coolest day with 29°C. (superlative degree)
questions.explanation: This uses the superlative degree of comparison ('coolest'). The superlative is used when comparing one thing with ALL others in a group to find the extreme (highest or lowest). Wednesday had the lowest temperature of 29°C among all seven days. We cannot use the positive degree ('as cool as') here because the question is not about equality between two days — it asks for the single coolest day. We cannot use the comparative degree ('cooler than') here because the question is not comparing two specific days — it asks for the coolest among ALL seven days. The superlative pattern is: X is the [adjective]-est / the most [adjective].

questions.question:
[Degrees of Comparison - Temperature Chart] Temperature chart: Sun=38°C, Mon=33°C, Tue=30°C, Wed=29°C, Thu=32°C, Fri=35°C, Sat=38°C

Which were the hottest days?
questions.answer: Sunday and Saturday were the hottest days with 38°C. (superlative degree)
questions.explanation: This uses the superlative degree of comparison ('hottest'). Both Sunday and Saturday had the highest temperature of 38°C, making them the hottest days of the week. When two or more items share the highest value, they can all take the superlative. We cannot use the positive degree here because the question is not about equality — it asks for the extreme. We cannot use the comparative degree here because we are not comparing just two days — we are finding the highest among all seven. Note: Even though two days share the top spot, we still use the superlative because we are identifying them as the highest in the entire group.

questions.question:
[Degrees of Comparison - Temperature Chart] Temperature chart: Sun=38°C, Mon=33°C, Tue=30°C, Wed=29°C, Thu=32°C, Fri=35°C, Sat=38°C

Which two days were cooler than Thursday?
questions.answer: Tuesday (30°C) and Wednesday (29°C) were cooler than Thursday (32°C). (comparative degree)
questions.explanation: This uses the comparative degree of comparison ('cooler than'). The comparative degree compares two things or sets to show which is more or less. Thursday was 32°C, and both Tuesday (30°C) and Wednesday (29°C) had lower temperatures, making them cooler than Thursday. We cannot use the positive degree ('as cool as') here because the question asks for days that are cooler, not equal. We cannot use the superlative degree ('coolest') here because the question is not about finding the single coolest day overall — it is comparing specific days to Thursday. The comparative pattern is: X is [adjective]-er than Y / more [adjective] than Y.

questions.question:
[Degrees of Comparison - Temperature Chart] Temperature chart: Sun=38°C, Mon=33°C, Tue=30°C, Wed=29°C, Thu=32°C, Fri=35°C, Sat=38°C

Which two days were hotter than Friday?
questions.answer: Sunday (38°C) and Saturday (38°C) were hotter than Friday (35°C). (comparative degree)
questions.explanation: This uses the comparative degree of comparison ('hotter than'). Friday was 35°C, and both Sunday (38°C) and Saturday (38°C) had higher temperatures, making them hotter than Friday. We cannot use the positive degree ('as hot as') here because the question asks for days that are hotter, not equal. We cannot use the superlative degree ('hottest') here because the question is specifically comparing days to Friday, not finding the overall hottest day. The word 'than' in the question signals that the comparative degree is needed.

questions.question: [Degrees of Comparison - Sentences] Write sentences using the positive, comparative, and superlative degrees of the adjective: intelligent
questions.answer:
Positive: Chimpanzees are intelligent animals.
Comparative: Bottlenose dolphins are more intelligent than chimpanzees.
Superlative: Orangutans are the most intelligent among animal species.
questions.explanation: 'Intelligent' is a multi-syllable adjective (four syllables: in-tel-li-gent), so it uses 'more' for comparative and 'most' for superlative. We cannot add '-er' or '-est' to form 'intelligenter' or 'intelligentest' — these do not exist in English. Only short adjectives (one syllable, or two syllables ending in '-y') take '-er'/'-est'. The positive degree ('intelligent') describes the quality without any comparison. The comparative ('more intelligent than') compares two things. The superlative ('the most intelligent') compares one thing with all others in a group.

questions.question: [Degrees of Comparison - Sentences] Write sentences using the positive, comparative, and superlative degrees of the adjective: healthy
questions.answer:
Positive: Fruits are healthy snacks.
Comparative: Vegetables are healthier than fried snacks.
Superlative: Green leafy vegetables are the healthiest food items.
questions.explanation: 'Healthy' is a two-syllable adjective ending in '-y', so it follows a special rule: change '-y' to '-ier' for comparative (healthier) and '-iest' for superlative (healthiest). We cannot use 'more healthy' or 'most healthy' as the primary form — while sometimes heard, 'healthier' and 'healthiest' are the standard forms for adjectives ending in '-y'. We also cannot simply add '-er'/'-est' without changing the 'y' — 'healthyer' and 'healthyest' are incorrect spellings. The '-y' must change to '-i' before adding the suffix. Same pattern: happy → happier → happiest, easy → easier → easiest.

questions.question: [Degrees of Comparison - Sentences] Write sentences using the positive, comparative, and superlative degrees of the adjective: powerful
questions.answer:
Positive: The lion is a powerful animal.
Comparative: The elephant is more powerful than the lion.
Superlative: The blue whale is the most powerful creature on Earth.
questions.explanation: 'Powerful' is a multi-syllable adjective (three syllables: pow-er-ful), so it uses 'more' for comparative and 'most' for superlative. We cannot add '-er' or '-est' to form 'powerfuler' or 'powerfulest' — these are incorrect in English. Long adjectives (three or more syllables) always use 'more/most'. We also cannot say 'powerful' in all three degrees — the comparative must show a higher level ('more powerful') and the superlative must show the highest level ('most powerful'). The positive degree states the quality as it is.

questions.question: [Degrees of Comparison - Sentences] Write sentences using the positive, comparative, and superlative degrees of the adjective: delicious
questions.answer:
Positive: My mother makes delicious biryani.
Comparative: Grandmother's biryani is more delicious than restaurant biryani.
Superlative: My mother's biryani is the most delicious biryani I have ever tasted.
questions.explanation: 'Delicious' is a multi-syllable adjective (four syllables: de-li-ci-ous), so it uses 'more' for comparative and 'most' for superlative. We cannot add '-er' or '-est' to form 'deliciouser' or 'deliciousest' — these are not valid English words. The rule is firm: adjectives with three or more syllables ALWAYS use 'more' and 'most'. We cannot use 'less/least' either, because those would show decreasing degrees (less delicious = not as tasty), which is the opposite direction.

questions.question: [Degrees of Comparison - Sentences] Write sentences using the positive, comparative, and superlative degrees of the adjective: important
questions.answer:
Positive: Exercise is important for good health.
Comparative: A balanced diet is more important than supplements.
Superlative: Good health is the most important thing in life.
questions.explanation: 'Important' is a multi-syllable adjective (three syllables: im-por-tant), so it uses 'more' for comparative and 'most' for superlative. We cannot add '-er' or '-est' to form 'importanter' or 'importantest' — these do not exist. Long adjectives always require 'more/most'. The positive degree describes the quality ('important') without comparing. The comparative ('more important than') compares two things (diet vs supplements). The superlative ('the most important') identifies the highest in a group (health above all else).

questions.question: [Degrees of Comparison - Sentences] Write sentences using the positive, comparative, and superlative degrees of the adjective: shy
questions.answer:
Positive: The new student is shy.
Comparative: My sister is shyer than my brother.
Superlative: Among all my cousins, Meera is the shyest.
questions.explanation: 'Shy' is a one-syllable adjective, so it adds '-er' for comparative (shyer) and '-est' for superlative (shyest). We cannot use 'more shy' or 'most shy' as the standard form — while occasionally heard in informal speech, 'shyer' and 'shyest' are the grammatically correct forms for one-syllable adjectives. One-syllable adjectives follow the simple '-er'/'-est' pattern, NOT the 'more/most' pattern. Other one-syllable examples: tall → taller → tallest, fast → faster → fastest, big → bigger → biggest.

questions.question:
[Interchange of Degrees] Change the degree of comparison while keeping the meaning the same:
Few countries in the world are as large as China. (Change to comparative and superlative)
questions.answer:
Comparative: China is larger than most of the other countries in the world.
Superlative: China is one of the largest countries in the world.
questions.explanation: The original sentence uses the positive degree ('as large as') with the word 'few', meaning not many countries equal China in size — but some do, so China is not THE largest. In the comparative conversion, we say 'larger than MOST other' (not 'any other') because 'few' means some countries ARE as large — so China is not larger than ALL others. We cannot say 'larger than any other' because that would mean China is the absolute largest, which contradicts 'few' (a few countries are equally large). In the superlative, we say 'ONE OF the largest' (not 'THE largest') for the same reason — China shares the top spot with a few others. The pattern: 'Few X are as ___ as Y' = 'Y is ___er than most other X' = 'Y is one of the ___est X'.

questions.question:
[Interchange of Degrees] Change the degree of comparison while keeping the meaning the same:
No other metal is as heavy as lead. (Change to comparative and superlative)
questions.answer:
Comparative: Lead is heavier than any other metal.
Superlative: Lead is the heaviest metal.
questions.explanation: The original uses the positive degree ('No other... as heavy as'), which means lead stands alone at the top — nothing equals it. Because of 'No other', the comparative must use 'any other' (not 'most other'), and the superlative must use 'THE heaviest' (not 'one of the heaviest'). We cannot say 'heavier than most other metals' because 'No other' means there are ZERO metals that match lead, not just a few. We cannot say 'one of the heaviest' because that would imply other metals are equally heavy, contradicting 'No other'. The pattern: 'No other X is as ___ as Y' = 'Y is ___er than any other X' = 'Y is the ___est X'. This is the strongest pattern — absolute superiority.

questions.question:
[Interchange of Degrees] Change the degree of comparison while keeping the meaning the same:
Rabindranath Tagore was the greatest poet of India. (Change to positive and comparative)
questions.answer:
Positive: No other poet of India was as great as Rabindranath Tagore.
Comparative: Rabindranath Tagore was greater than any other poet of India.
questions.explanation: The original uses the superlative degree ('the greatest'), meaning Tagore stands alone at the top — no one equals him. For the positive, we must use 'No other... as great as' (not 'Few... as great as') because 'the greatest' means absolute superiority. We cannot use 'Very few poets were as great' because that would imply some others were equally great, contradicting 'the greatest'. For the comparative, we must use 'any other' (not 'most other') because Tagore surpasses everyone. We cannot use 'greater than some poets' because that would weaken the meaning. The word 'other' is essential — 'greater than any poet' would compare Tagore to himself, which is illogical.

questions.question:
[Interchange of Degrees] Change the degree of comparison while keeping the meaning the same:
The Taj Mahal is more beautiful than many other monuments. (Change to positive and superlative)
questions.answer:
Positive: Very few monuments are as beautiful as the Taj Mahal.
Superlative: The Taj Mahal is one of the most beautiful monuments.
questions.explanation: The original uses the comparative degree ('more beautiful than many other'), meaning the Taj Mahal is near the top but not necessarily THE single most beautiful — it surpasses many but perhaps not all. Because of 'many other', the positive must use 'Very few' (not 'No other'), and the superlative must use 'one of the most' (not 'the most'). We cannot say 'No other monument is as beautiful' because 'many other' implies some monuments might still equal the Taj Mahal. We cannot say 'the most beautiful monument' because that would mean it is the absolute best, which the original does not claim. The pattern: '___er than many other X' = 'Very few X are as ___ as Y' = 'Y is one of the ___est X'.

questions.question:
[Interchange of Degrees] Change the degree of comparison while keeping the meaning the same:
India is the largest democracy in the world. (Change to positive and comparative)
questions.answer:
Positive: No other democracy in the world is as large as India.
Comparative: India is larger than any other democracy in the world.
questions.explanation: The original uses the superlative ('the largest'), meaning India stands alone — no other democracy equals its size. For the positive, we must use 'No other' (not 'Few' or 'Very few') because 'the largest' means absolute superiority. We cannot use 'Very few democracies are as large' because that would imply some others match India, contradicting 'the largest'. For the comparative, we must use 'any other' (not 'most other') because India surpasses all. The word 'other' is important — we say 'any other democracy' because India is itself a democracy, and we cannot compare it to itself.

questions.question:
[Interchange of Degrees] Change the degree of comparison while keeping the meaning the same:
Soufia is the most studious student in the class. (Change to positive and comparative)
questions.answer:
Positive: No other student in the class is as studious as Soufia.
Comparative: Soufia is more studious than any other student in the class.
questions.explanation: The original uses the superlative ('the most studious'), meaning Soufia is at the absolute top. For the positive, we must use 'No other' because 'the most studious' means no one equals her. We cannot use 'Few students are as studious' because that would weaken the meaning. For the comparative, we use 'more studious' (not 'studiouser') because 'studious' is a multi-syllable adjective requiring 'more/most'. We also use 'any other student' (not 'most other students') to maintain the meaning of absolute superiority. The word 'other' is needed because Soufia is herself a student in the class.

questions.question:
[Interchange of Degrees] Change the degree of comparison while keeping the meaning the same:
This is the safest route to escape from the country. (Change to positive and comparative)
questions.answer:
Positive: No other route to escape from the country is as safe as this.
Comparative: This route is safer than any other route to escape from the country.
questions.explanation: The original uses the superlative ('the safest'), meaning this route stands alone at the top — no other route is as safe. For the positive, we must use 'No other' because 'the safest' means absolute superiority. We cannot use 'Few routes are as safe' because that would imply other routes match this one. For the comparative, we use 'safer' (adding '-er' because 'safe' is a one-syllable adjective). We cannot use 'more safe' because one-syllable adjectives take '-er', not 'more'. We use 'any other route' to maintain the meaning that this route surpasses all others.

questions.question:
[Interchange of Degrees] Change the degree of comparison while keeping the meaning the same:
The pen can be mightier than any other tool for fighting against social evils. (Change to positive and superlative)
questions.answer:
Positive: No other tool for fighting against social evils can be as mighty as the pen.
Superlative: The pen can be the mightiest tool for fighting against social evils.
questions.explanation: The original uses the comparative ('mightier than any other'), which has the same meaning as 'the mightiest' — when the comparative uses 'any other', it means absolute superiority. For the positive, we use 'No other' (not 'Few' or 'Very few') because 'than any other' means the pen surpasses everything — no tool equals it. We cannot use 'Very few tools are as mighty' because that would suggest some tools match the pen. For the superlative, we use 'the mightiest' (adding '-est' because 'mighty' changes from '-y' to '-iest'). We cannot use 'one of the mightiest' because the original claims absolute superiority. Note: 'can be' is retained in all forms to preserve the meaning of possibility.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
The ______ (origin) Olympic Games began in ancient Greece.
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-al' to 'origin' forms the adjective 'original', meaning 'relating to the beginning or earliest form'. The sentence needs an adjective to describe what kind of Olympic Games — the first ones, the ones from the beginning. We cannot use 'origin' as is because it is a noun, and we need an adjective before the noun 'Olympic Games'. We cannot use 'originate' because that is a verb. We cannot use 'originally' because that is an adverb (it would modify a verb, not a noun). The suffix '-al' creates an adjective meaning 'relating to' — origin → original, nation → national.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
They were a set of ______ (athlete) competitions.
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-ic' to 'athlete' (dropping the final 'e') forms the adjective 'athletic', meaning 'relating to athletes or athletics'. The sentence needs an adjective to describe what kind of competitions. We cannot use 'athlete' as is because it is a noun (a person), not an adjective. We cannot use 'athletically' because that is an adverb. We cannot use 'athleted' because that is not a real word. The suffix '-ic' creates adjectives meaning 'relating to' — athlete → athletic, hero → heroic, poet → poetic.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
The games had ______ (religion) significance.
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-ous' to 'religion' (with spelling adjustment to '-ious') forms the adjective 'religious', meaning 'relating to religion'. The sentence needs an adjective to describe what kind of significance. We cannot use 'religion' as is because it is a noun, not an adjective — 'religion significance' is grammatically incorrect. We cannot use 'religiously' because that is an adverb. We cannot use 'religional' because it is not a standard word. The suffix '-ous'/'-ious' creates adjectives — religion → religious, fame → famous, danger → dangerous.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
Hosting the Olympics is an ______ (expense) affair.
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-ive' to 'expense' (with spelling change — dropping 'e' and adding '-ive') forms the adjective 'expensive', meaning 'costing a lot of money'. The sentence needs an adjective to describe what kind of affair. We cannot use 'expense' as is because it is a noun — 'an expense affair' is grammatically incorrect. We cannot use 'expensively' because that is an adverb. We cannot use 'expenseful' because it is not a word. The suffix '-ive' creates adjectives — expense → expensive, attract → attractive, create → creative.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
The Olympics include various ______ (culture) events.
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-al' to 'culture' (dropping the final 'e') forms the adjective 'cultural', meaning 'relating to culture'. The sentence needs an adjective to describe what kind of events. We cannot use 'culture' as is because it is a noun — 'culture events' is not standard English (though informally used). We cannot use 'culturally' because that is an adverb. We cannot use 'culturous' or 'cultureish' because these do not exist. The suffix '-al' creates adjectives of relation — culture → cultural, nature → natural, structure → structural.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
It is the greatest ______ (sport) event in the world.
questions.explanation: The noun 'sport' can become the adjective 'sporting' (by adding '-ing') or 'sports' (used as a noun modifier). Both forms are correct and commonly used. 'Sporting event' and 'sports event' both mean an event related to sports. We cannot use 'sport' alone before 'event' as a single adjective in formal English — 'sport event' sounds incomplete. We cannot use 'sportful' or 'sportive' because 'sportful' does not exist, and while 'sportive' is a real word, it means 'playful', not 'related to sports'. We cannot use 'sportily' because that is an adverb.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
The marathon today is ______ (long) than the original race.
questions.explanation: The comparative degree of 'long' is 'longer' (formed by adding '-er'). The word 'than' in the sentence is the key signal — it tells us a comparison is being made between two things (today's marathon vs the original race), so the comparative degree is needed. We cannot use 'long' (positive degree) because the sentence compares two things with 'than', which requires the comparative form. We cannot use 'longest' (superlative) because we are comparing only two things, not finding the extreme among many. We cannot use 'more long' because 'long' is a one-syllable adjective and takes '-er', not 'more'.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
There were ______ (few) events in the ancient games.
questions.explanation: The comparative degree of 'few' is 'fewer' (formed by adding '-er'). The context implies a comparison between the ancient games and modern games — the ancient games had a smaller number of events. We cannot use 'few' (positive degree) alone because the sentence implies a comparison, and we need to show that the number was lower compared to something else. We cannot use 'fewest' (superlative) because we are comparing two things (ancient vs modern), not finding the lowest among many. We cannot use 'less' because 'less' is used with uncountable nouns, while 'fewer' is used with countable nouns like 'events'.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
The Olympics is the ______ (popular) sporting event.
questions.explanation: The superlative degree of 'popular' is 'most popular'. The word 'the' before the blank signals that the superlative degree is needed — 'the ___est' or 'the most ___' is the superlative pattern. We cannot use 'popular' (positive) because 'the popular sporting event' does not convey the superlative meaning — it does not say it is the number one event. We cannot use 'more popular' (comparative) because 'the more popular sporting event' would need a 'than' clause to compare with something specific. We cannot use 'popularest' because 'popular' is a three-syllable adjective and must use 'most', not '-est'.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
It is the ______ (great) show of unity among nations.
questions.explanation: The superlative degree of 'great' is 'greatest' (formed by adding '-est'). The word 'the' before the blank signals that the superlative degree is needed. We cannot use 'great' (positive) because 'the great show' would not convey the meaning of the highest degree — just that it is a great show, not THE greatest. We cannot use 'greater' (comparative) because 'the greater show' would need a 'than' clause. We cannot use 'most great' because 'great' is a one-syllable adjective and takes '-est', not 'most'. One-syllable rule: great → greater → greatest.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
The athletes compete in a ______ (peace) environment.
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-ful' to 'peace' forms the adjective 'peaceful', meaning 'calm and free from disturbance'. The sentence needs an adjective to describe what kind of environment the athletes compete in. We cannot use 'peace' as is because it is a noun — 'a peace environment' is grammatically incorrect. We cannot use 'peacefully' because that is an adverb (it would modify a verb, not a noun). We cannot use 'peaceless' because it would mean 'without peace', which is the opposite of the intended meaning. The suffix '-ful' means 'full of' — peaceful = full of peace.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
They are ______ (fear) competitors.
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-less' to 'fear' forms the adjective 'fearless', meaning 'without fear' or 'brave'. The sentence describes athletes who compete without being afraid — they are brave competitors. We cannot use 'fearful' because that means 'full of fear' or 'frightened', which is the opposite of the intended meaning — fearful competitors would be scared, not brave. We cannot use 'fear' as is because it is a noun/verb, not an adjective. We cannot use 'feared' because 'feared competitors' would mean competitors who are feared by others (a different meaning from being brave). The suffix '-less' means 'without' — fearless = without fear.

questions.question:
[Complete the Paragraph] Fill in the correct form of the word in brackets:
The Olympic spirit is truly ______ (compete).
questions.explanation: Adding the suffix '-itive' to 'compete' (dropping the final 'e') forms the adjective 'competitive', meaning 'involving or characterized by competition'. The sentence needs an adjective after the linking verb 'is' to describe the Olympic spirit. We cannot use 'compete' as is because it is a verb — 'The spirit is truly compete' is grammatically incorrect. We cannot use 'competing' because 'The spirit is truly competing' would change the meaning to the spirit actively taking part in something. We cannot use 'competitively' because that is an adverb. The suffix '-itive' creates an adjective from the verb — compete → competitive, sense → sensitive.

questions.question:
[Degrees of Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct degree of the adjective in brackets:
There may be ______ (good) performers than Nagesh in our city.
questions.explanation: 'Better' is the irregular comparative form of 'good'. The word 'than' in the sentence signals that a comparison is being made between two things (other performers vs Nagesh), so the comparative degree is needed. We cannot use 'good' (positive) because the sentence uses 'than', which requires a comparative form. We cannot use 'best' (superlative) because we are comparing Nagesh to other performers, not identifying the single best among all. We cannot use 'gooder' because 'good' is an irregular adjective — it does not follow the '-er' rule. The irregular forms are: good → better → best.

questions.question:
[Degrees of Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct degree of the adjective in brackets:
I find the game of football ______ (exciting) than cricket.
questions.explanation: 'More exciting' is the comparative form of 'exciting'. The word 'than' signals a comparison between football and cricket, so the comparative degree is needed. We cannot use 'exciting' (positive) because 'than' requires a comparative form — 'exciting than cricket' is grammatically incorrect. We cannot use 'most exciting' (superlative) because we are comparing just two sports, not finding the best among many. We cannot use 'excitinger' because 'exciting' is a three-syllable adjective and must use 'more', not '-er'. Multi-syllable adjectives always form the comparative with 'more'.

questions.question:
[Degrees of Adjectives] Fill in the blanks with the correct degree of the adjectives in brackets:
I have solved many ______ (difficult) riddles, but this one was the ______ (complex).
questions.explanation: First blank: 'difficult' in the positive degree because no comparison is being made — the sentence simply states that many riddles were difficult. We cannot use 'more difficult' here because there is no 'than' and no comparison happening in this part. We cannot use 'most difficult' because the sentence is not identifying one riddle as the hardest here. Second blank: 'most complex' in the superlative degree because the word 'the' before the blank and the context (comparing this one riddle to all others) signal the superlative. We cannot use 'complex' (positive) because 'the complex' does not convey the meaning of being the absolute hardest. We cannot use 'more complex' (comparative) because there is no 'than' — the sentence means this riddle was the hardest of all.

questions.question:
[Degrees of Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct degree of the adjective in brackets:
It will be remembered as the ______ (bad) day in the history of the world.
questions.explanation: 'Worst' is the irregular superlative form of 'bad'. The phrase 'the ___' and 'in the history of the world' signal that the superlative degree is needed — we are comparing this day to ALL other days in history to identify the most extreme. We cannot use 'bad' (positive) because 'the bad day in history' does not convey the meaning of being the single most terrible day. We cannot use 'worse' (comparative) because we are not comparing two specific days — we are saying it is the worst among all. We cannot use 'baddest' because 'bad' is an irregular adjective — it does not follow the '-est' rule. The irregular forms are: bad → worse → worst.

questions.question:
[Degrees of Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct degree of the adjective in brackets:
To say the world is coming to an end is a ______ (silly) thing.
questions.explanation: The positive degree 'silly' is used because NO comparison is being made. The sentence simply describes the statement as a silly thing — it does not compare it to anything else. We cannot use 'sillier' (comparative) because there is no 'than' and no second thing being compared. We cannot use 'silliest' (superlative) because there is no 'the' before the blank and no group being compared — the sentence uses 'a', not 'the', which confirms it is not superlative. The positive degree is used when we simply describe a quality without comparing it to anything.

questions.question:
[Degrees of Adjectives] Fill in the blank with the correct degree of the adjective in brackets:
Today, she is a ______ (celebrated) artist than her mother.
questions.explanation: 'More celebrated' is the comparative form of 'celebrated'. The word 'than' signals a comparison between two people (the artist and her mother), so the comparative degree is needed. We cannot use 'celebrated' (positive) because 'than' requires a comparative form — 'a celebrated artist than her mother' is grammatically incorrect. We cannot use 'most celebrated' (superlative) because we are comparing only two people (daughter vs mother), not finding the best among many. We cannot use 'celebrateder' because 'celebrated' is a multi-syllable adjective and must use 'more', not '-er'. Multi-syllable adjectives always use 'more' for the comparative.
